title: Papering a ceiling
subtitle: Hang lining paper or wallpaper on a ceiling to a clean finish
excerpt: How to hang lining paper or wallpaper across a ceiling, including pasting and booking the lengths, setting up a safe working platform, working from a plumb starting line, overlapping the lengths at the walls, and avoiding the most common cause of dropped lengths and visible joins.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Dulux decorator guides. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

glossaryTerms.definition: Folding a pasted length of wallpaper paste-to-paste, without creasing, so that the paste soaks into the paper and the paper relaxes before hanging. Ceiling paper is typically folded into three or four equal sections. Booking makes a long pasted length manageable and prevents paste drying on the surface.

A plain buff or white paper hung on ceilings and walls before painting or decorating. It provides a smooth, stable surface that conceals minor imperfections and prevents successive paint coats from pulling at each other. Available in grades 800 to 2000; 1200 is the standard ceiling weight.

A taut string coated with powdered chalk that, when snapped against a surface, leaves a straight line over a long distance. Used to mark a straight starting line across a ceiling, since the ceiling-to-wall junction is rarely straight enough to use as a reference.

content.text: Papering a ceiling is harder than papering a wall for one reason: gravity works against you throughout the job. A pasted length of

weighs more than a wall length of the same material and has nothing to rest against while you position it. A proper working platform: planks between two stepladders or a hired hop-up: is not optional.
the pasted length into concertina folds is what makes handling a ceiling length alone possible.

Fill any cracks with a flexible filler and sand flush when dry. Wash off any grease or mould with a sugar-soap solution. If there is existing wallpaper, wet and strip it completely: paper will not bond to paper. Seal bare plaster with a diluted PVA coat (1 part PVA to 4 parts water) or a proprietary size to prevent the ceiling drawing moisture from the paste too fast.

Work at right angles to the main window so that the joins between lengths fall parallel to the light rather than across it. Measure 500 mm in from one side wall along the length of the room, mark at both ends, and snap a
between the two marks. This is the guiding edge for the first length. Starting exactly at the side wall is unreliable because walls are rarely perfectly straight.
Step 3, cut and paste the lengths

Cut lengths about 100 mm longer than the room width: 50 mm spare at each end. Lay the length on the paste table, paste thoroughly (work paste well into the edges), and book it by folding paste-to-paste in accordion folds of about 400 mm each. Set each booked length to soak for the time given on the packet: usually 3 to 5 minutes for standard lining paper.

Carry the booked length to the ceiling platform. Unfold the first section and press it to the ceiling, aligning the edge with the chalk line. Slide the length until the edge is exactly on the line, then press and smooth from the centre outward using the paint brush. Unfold the next booked section and smooth it in place, continuing to the far wall. Allow 50 mm of overlap onto each side wall; trim with the utility knife against a straightedge once the whole length is in place.

Butt each successive length tightly against the previous one; do not overlap. Work from the chalk-line side toward the far wall. Check that the butt joins remain tight as each length goes up: the weight of a long wet length can pull a loose join open before the paste grabs. If the join opens, run a seam roller lightly along it while the paste is still wet.

Ready-pasted lining paper is available for smaller ceilings: soak in a water tray, book briefly, and apply without separate pasting. It is slower on large ceilings because the paste activates unevenly. For a painted ceiling, lining paper at grade 1200 is sufficient. For ceilings that will be papered with a heavyweight decorative wallpaper, use grade 2000 lining paper as a base.

attrs.symptom: Length drops from the ceiling before it can be smoothed
attrs.cause: Paste too thin; paper not soaked long enough; ceiling not sized so it absorbs paste too fast.
attrs.fix: Apply a size coat to the ceiling the day before papering. Mix paste to the thickness recommended for ceiling work (heavier than wall paste). Allow booked lengths to soak for the full recommended time.

attrs.cause: Butt joins not tight; or lengths overlapped rather than butted.
attrs.fix: Apply a skim coat of fine filler over the join with a wide filling knife, sand smooth, and repaint. Ensure all joins are butt joints, not overlaps.

attrs.cause: Air trapped under the paper during smoothing; or wet paper applied to an unsealed porous surface that shrinks as it dries.
attrs.fix: Small bubbles usually disappear as the paper dries. Large bubbles that remain after 24 hours: pierce with a pin, inject a small amount of paste with a syringe, and press flat with a damp cloth.
